questions.question:
In the given figure, PQRSTV is a regular hexagon with each side of length 5 cm. A circle is drawn with its centre at V such that it passes through P. What is the area (in cm^2) of the shaded region? (The diagram is representative)

A. 25pi/3
B. 20pi/3
C. 6pi
D. 7pi
questions.to_find: area of the shaded region formed by the circle sector inside the hexagon at vertex V

understand.plain: A regular hexagon PQRSTV has all sides equal to 5 cm and all interior angles equal to 120 degrees. A circle is centred at vertex V with radius equal to the distance VP. Since V and P are adjacent vertices, VP = 5 cm. The shaded region is the circular sector at V that lies inside the hexagon, which is exactly the sector subtending the interior angle at V.

keywords.explain: A six-sided polygon with all sides equal and all interior angles equal to 120 degrees.
keywords.example: PQRSTV with each side 5 cm — every interior angle is 120 degrees.

keywords.explain: The pie-slice region bounded by two radii and an arc of a circle.
keywords.example: At vertex V with radius 5 and angle 120 degrees, the sector area is (120/360) * pi * 25 = 25pi/3.

keywords.explain: Each interior angle of a regular hexagon = (6-2)*180/6 = 120 degrees.
keywords.example: At vertex V the two sides VP and VT both have length 5 and the included angle is 120 degrees.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 320 300" width="320" height="300"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="320" height="300" class="bg" rx="10"/><polygon points="160,40 230,80 230,160 160,200 90,160 90,80" fill="none" stroke="var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M90,80 A70,70 0 0,0 90,160" fill="var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)" stroke="var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.85"/><line x1="90" y1="80" x2="90" y2="80" stroke="none"/><text x="75" y="75" class="lbl ink">P</text><text x="234" y="75" class="lbl ink">Q</text><text x="238" y="168" class="lbl ink">R</text><text x="155" y="218" class="lbl ink">S</text><text x="62" y="168" class="lbl ink">T</text><text x="62" y="78" class="lbl ink">V</text><text x="100" y="125" class="tag mut">120deg</text><text x="95" y="145" class="sub mut">sector</text><text x="130" y="265" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">side = 5 cm, radius = VP = 5 cm</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Regular hexagon PQRSTV with a shaded sector at vertex V (top-left), swept from side VP to side VT at 120 degrees, radius 5 cm.

given.aim: find the area of the circular sector at vertex V inside the hexagon

terms.meaning: all six sides of the regular hexagon are 5 cm each
terms.example: PQ = QR = RS = ST = TV = VP = 5 cm
terms.connects: determines the radius of the circle since V and P are adjacent vertices

terms.meaning: the circle is centred at V and passes through P, so r = VP = side length = 5 cm
terms.example: In the hexagon PQRSTV the vertices V and P are adjacent, so VP = 5 cm
terms.connects: used directly in the sector area formula

terms.meaning: each interior angle of a regular hexagon equals (6-2)*180/6 = 120 degrees
terms.example: angle PVT = 120 degrees — the sector sweeps from side VP to side VT
terms.connects: determines the fraction of the full circle that forms the shaded sector

given.plan: Identify the shaded region as the 120-degree sector of a circle of radius 5 at vertex V. Use the sector area formula: (theta/360) * pi * r^2 with theta = 120 and r = 5.

steps.title: Identify the radius of the circle
steps.apply: In hexagon PQRSTV the vertices V and P are consecutive (adjacent). Adjacent vertices are separated by one side. So VP = s = 5 cm. The circle centred at V passes through P, hence radius r = 5 cm.
steps.note: Do not confuse VP with the long diagonal (which equals 2s = 10 cm). Adjacent vertices give one side length.

steps.title: Determine the sector angle at V
steps.formula_raw: interior angle = (n-2)*180/n degrees for a regular n-gon
steps.apply: For n = 6: interior angle = (6-2)*180/6 = 4*180/6 = 120 degrees. The sector at V spans from radius VP to radius VT (the two sides meeting at V), so the sector angle theta = 120 degrees.
steps.note: The shaded region is exactly this sector — no other areas need to be added or subtracted.

questions.formula_note: Three new formulas are registered: sector area, regular polygon interior angle, and the adjacent-vertex distance property of a regular hexagon.

questions.question:
What will be the maximum sum of 44, 42, 40, ... ?

A. 502
B. 504
C. 506
D. 500
questions.to_find: maximum possible sum of the arithmetic series 44, 42, 40, ...

understand.plain: The series 44, 42, 40, ... is an arithmetic progression with first term 44 and common difference -2. The terms decrease by 2 each time and eventually become negative. Adding more negative terms reduces the total sum, so the maximum sum is achieved just before the terms turn negative — that is, by summing all non-negative terms.

keywords.explain: A sequence where consecutive terms differ by a constant value called the common difference d.
Since d < 0, terms decrease. The sum increases as long as terms are positive and decreases once terms become negative. Maximum sum = sum of all positive terms.
keywords.example: Terms: 44, 42, ..., 2, 0, -2, ... Stop summing at the last non-negative term (which is 0 or the last positive term).

keywords.term: sum of n terms of an AP
keywords.explain: S_n = n/2 * (2a + (n-1)d) = n/2 * (first term + last term).

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a purely numerical series question.

given.aim: find the number of terms that maximises the sum, then compute that sum

terms.connects: used in the nth-term formula T_n = a + (n-1)d to find when terms become negative

terms.meaning: each term is 2 less than the previous term
terms.connects: since d < 0 the series is decreasing; the sum is maximised just before terms go negative

terms.meaning: the term where T_n >= 0 but T_{n+1} < 0 marks the cutoff for maximum sum
terms.connects: including the zero term (T_23 = 0) does not change the sum, so S_22 = S_23 = 506

given.plan: Find the last non-negative term using T_n = a + (n-1)d >= 0. Then apply S_n = n/2*(first + last) for that many terms.

steps.title: Find the last non-negative term
steps.note: T_23 = 0 contributes nothing to the sum; maximum sum is achieved at n = 22 or n = 23 (both give the same value).

steps.title: Compute the maximum sum
steps.note: Adding the zero term at n = 23 does not change the sum, confirming the maximum is 506.

questions.formula_note: Two new AP formulas are registered: the nth-term formula and the sum of n terms formula for an arithmetic progression.

formulas.name: Area of a Circular Sector
formulas.plain: Area = (theta/360) * pi * r^2  where theta is the central angle in degrees and r is the radius

formulas.name: Interior Angle of a Regular n-gon
Adjacent Vertex Distance in a Regular Hexagon
formulas.plain: distance between any two adjacent vertices = side length s; long diagonal = 2s

formulas.name: nth Term of an Arithmetic Progression
formulas.plain: T_n = a + (n - 1) * d  where a = first term, d = common difference

formulas.name: Sum of First n Terms of an Arithmetic Progression
formulas.plain: S_n = n/2 * (2a + (n-1)*d) = n/2 * (a + T_n)  where T_n is the nth term
